session_why.text: This session targets lower body power development through the combination of heavy back squat work (ATP-PC system, neural adaptation) followed by a glycolytic metcon emphasizing explosive lower body movements. The squat-first sequencing ensures CNS freshness for maximal force production. The 12-minute AMRAP keeps the time domain in the glycolytic zone (10 sec - 2 min high-intensity efforts) without excessive oxidative demand that would interfere with strength adaptation.
session_why.source: Wilson et al 2012 - Concurrent Training Meta-Analysis
session_why.url_or_ref: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research 26(8)

movement_why.text: Back squat is the primary lower body strength movement, developing force production in the squat pattern. Box jumps develop rate of force development (power), kettlebell swings train the hinge pattern with explosive hip extension, and air squats provide volume under fatigue. This combination addresses three of Glassman's ten physical skills: strength, power, and stamina.

loading_why.text: The ramp/build-up progression scheme (5@65%, 4@72%, 3@78%, 2@83%, 2@85%) is appropriate for Week 1 baseline establishment and neural preparation. This progressive loading prepares the nervous system for heavier loads while allowing technique refinement under progressively challenging weights. Zatsiorsky & Kraemer describe progressive warm-up sets as essential for neural preparation before maximal efforts. The 180-second rest intervals allow phosphocreatine replenishment for strength work above 80% 1RM.
loading_why.source: Zatsiorsky & Kraemer - Science and Practice of Strength Training

session_why.text: This session emphasizes the hinge pattern through deadlift strength work and Romanian deadlift volume in the metcon. The E2MOM strength format maintains session flow while providing adequate phosphocreatine recovery between sets. The For Time metcon stays in the medium time domain (7-15 min), targeting the glycolytic system without excessive aerobic volume that would interfere with strength adaptation. Rowing provides monostructural cardio to balance the hinge-dominant movements.

movement_why.text: The deadlift is the foundational hinge pattern, developing posterior chain strength and total body force production. Romanian deadlift in the metcon provides eccentric loading and hamstring volume while reinforcing the hinge pattern under fatigue. This addresses movement balance requirements for weekly hinge pattern work. The rowing machine provides monostructural cardio while complementing the posterior chain emphasis through the pulling motion.

loading_why.text: The E2MOM format at 75% 1RM with 3 reps per round is appropriate for Week 2 building intensity while managing fatigue. The 2-minute interval allows sufficient phosphocreatine recovery for loads in the 70-85% range. This format creates auto-regulated rest - faster execution earns more recovery time, rewarding efficiency and maintaining engagement with the timer. Total volume of 8 sets x 3 reps = 24 reps falls within the 4-12 set range for hinge pattern weekly volume.

session_why.text: This session builds squat pattern volume through front squats, which emphasize quadriceps and require greater core stability than back squats due to the anterior load position. The straight-set format at moderate intensity (72%) allows accumulation of quality volume for hypertrophy and work capacity. The 3-station EMOM metcon provides a glycolytic stimulus with lower body emphasis (goblet squats) while incorporating monostructural cardio and full-body conditioning.

movement_why.text: Front squat develops the squat pattern with greater emphasis on quadriceps and anterior core than back squat, providing movement variation within the squat pattern category. Goblet squats in the metcon reinforce the squat pattern under fatigue at lower loading. Assault bike provides monostructural cardio (required weekly), and burpees contribute to general physical preparedness through full-body, high-intensity movement.

loading_why.text: Straight sets at 72% 1RM for 5x5 is classic hypertrophy and baseline strength programming, appropriate for Week 1-2 phases. This scheme is simple to execute, builds work capacity at a specific intensity, and has lower cognitive load than complex progression schemes. The 120-second rest intervals are appropriate for the hypertrophy zone (60-90 seconds is standard, but 120 seconds accommodates the compound nature of front squats). Total volume of 25 reps at 72% provides significant structural adaptation stimulus.
loading_why.source: Schoenfeld - Science and Development of Muscle Hypertrophy

session_why.text: This session targets lower body power through Olympic lifting (hang power clean) and plyometrics (box jumps). The wave loading in the strength block exploits post-activation potentiation to increase neural output. The 10-minute AMRAP stays in the short-to-medium time domain, emphasizing the glycolytic system while maintaining power output quality. Olympic lifting is placed on a fresh CNS day, not following heavy squats or deadlifts, per recovery sequencing rules.

movement_why.text: Hang power clean is an Olympic lift variation emphasizing explosive hip extension and rate of force development - addressing the 'power' physical skill. Box jumps develop lower body power through plyometric training. Power cleans in the metcon reinforce the movement pattern under fatigue at moderate loading. Double-unders provide coordination and ankle/calf power endurance. This combination develops power, coordination, and cardiovascular endurance per Glassman's ten physical skills.

loading_why.text: Wave loading (3@72%, 3@77%, 3@82%, 3@72%, 3@78%, 3@83%) is appropriate for Week 2-3 strength emphasis with experienced athletes. Robbins (2005) documents post-activation potentiation effects lasting 3-10 minutes after heavy loading - the heavier sets prime the nervous system for enhanced performance on subsequent moderate-load sets. This scheme allows higher total volume at elevated intensities while managing fatigue through the oscillating pattern. The 180-second rest ensures phosphocreatine recovery for power work.
loading_why.url_or_ref: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research 19(2)

strength.label: Back Squat - Top Set + Back-Off Volume

movements.name: Back Squat
movements.rest: 180 seconds after top set, 90 seconds between volume sets
movements.notes: Top set + back-off volume scheme. First set is heavy top set of 2 reps at 85% for neural stimulation. Then drop to 68% for 4 sets of 5 reps to accumulate hypertrophy volume. This combines peak intensity exposure with structural adaptation work in one efficient session.
movements.tempo: Top set: controlled and precise. Volume sets: consistent quality

session_why.text: This session develops squat pattern stamina through the combination of strength work and high-rep metcon squatting. The top set + back-off volume scheme efficiently combines neural (heavy top set) and structural (volume work) adaptations. The medium time domain metcon (11-15 min target) emphasizes the glycolytic-to-oxidative transition zone, building muscular endurance in the squat pattern while maintaining intensity below the aerobic threshold that would excessively interfere with strength gains.
session_why.source: Wilson et al 2012 - Concurrent Training Meta-Analysis
session_why.url_or_ref: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research 26(8)

movement_why.text: Back squat is the primary lower body strength movement. Goblet squats in the metcon provide high-rep squat pattern work under fatigue, developing the 'stamina' physical skill - the ability to process, deliver, store and use energy in repeated efforts. Ski erg provides monostructural cardio (required weekly) and complements the lower body emphasis. Kettlebell swings add hinge pattern work to balance the squat-dominant session.

loading_why.text: The top set + back-off volume scheme is appropriate for Week 3 peak intensity sessions with intermediate athletes. The heavy top set (2@85%) provides neural stimulation and peak intensity exposure while the athlete is fresh. Back-off sets at 68% (4x5) accumulate 20 reps of quality volume for hypertrophy without excessive CNS fatigue. Bompa & Haff describe this concurrent approach as maximizing adaptation diversity within a single session - neural work followed by structural work minimizes interference while addressing multiple training goals efficiently.

session_why.text: This session emphasizes unilateral (single-leg) lower body work to address asymmetries and develop balance and stability - two of Glassman's ten physical skills. Unilateral training reduces load on the spine while maintaining high muscular demand, provides variety from bilateral movements, and identifies strength imbalances between limbs. The 14-minute AMRAP stays in the medium time domain, targeting the glycolytic system while accumulating unilateral movement volume.

movement_why.text: Single-leg Romanian deadlift develops unilateral hinge pattern strength and balance. Bulgarian split squats target the squat pattern unilaterally with significant quad and glute demand. Box step-ups in the metcon provide explosive unilateral work. Single-arm KB swings add anti-rotation core demand while maintaining the hinge pattern. Rowing provides monostructural cardio (weekly requirement) and allows recovery between unilateral efforts. This session addresses balance, coordination, and strength skills concurrently.

loading_why.text: RPE 7-8 prescription is appropriate when training 1RM is unknown for accessory/unilateral movements. Unilateral work typically uses 50-70% of the load used for bilateral movements due to balance and stability demands. The 90-second rest between sets falls in the hypertrophy rest range, appropriate for the 8-10 rep schemes used. Four sets per movement provides 8 total working sets per leg across both exercises, which falls within weekly volume guidelines for lower body patterns while accounting for the unilateral nature reducing systemic fatigue compared to heavy bilateral work.

strength.label: Sumo Deadlift High Pull - EMOM Power

movements.name: Sumo Deadlift High Pull
movements.loading: E90s x 10 rounds: 5 SDHP @ moderate load (65-70% of deadlift 1RM or RPE 7)
movements.notes: Every 90 seconds for 10 rounds. SDHP emphasizes explosive hip extension and vertical pull - power development for posterior chain. Moderate load allows quality speed of movement. Total time: 15 minutes of work.
movements.tempo: Explosive from floor, fast pull to chest height

movements.name: Kettlebell Swing (American)
movements.notes: Full overhead swing - explosive hip extension to overhead
movements.scaling: Russian swing (eye level) or reduce weight

movements.name: Assault Bike
movements.notes: Descending calories: 15/12 in round 1, 12/9 in round 2, 9/6 in round 3

movements.name: Pigeon Pose
movements.notes: Deep hip and glute release after posterior chain volume

movements.name: Cat-Cow
movements.notes: Spinal decompression after hinge work

movements.name: Glute Bridge
movements.notes: Gentle glute activation and hip extension

session_why.text: This session targets posterior chain power and endurance through explosive hip extension movements. SDHP in the strength block develops rate of force development in the hinge pattern at moderate loads with high velocity. The short metcon (7-10 min target) stays in the glycolytic time domain, emphasizing power endurance with American KB swings (full hip extension to overhead). The descending rep scheme (21-15-9) maintains intensity as fatigue accumulates.

movement_why.text: Sumo deadlift high pull is a ballistic hinge movement emphasizing explosive hip extension and vertical pulling - developing power and speed (two of the ten physical skills). American kettlebell swings reinforce explosive hip extension with overhead stability demand. Assault bike provides monostructural cardio (weekly requirement) and complements the hip-dominant movements. This session addresses power, speed, and cardiovascular endurance concurrently.

loading_why.text: The E90s (every 90 seconds) EMOM format for 10 rounds with 5 reps of SDHP is appropriate for power development. The interval is short enough to maintain work density but provides adequate recovery between explosive sets. Load at 65-70% of deadlift 1RM or RPE 7 allows maximal velocity of movement - critical for power development. Zatsiorsky & Kraemer identify that power training requires moderate loads (30-70%) moved at maximal speed. The 90-second interval allows phosphocreatine recovery between power efforts. Total volume of 50 reps across 10 sets provides significant posterior chain stimulation without excessive loading that would compromise speed.
loading_why.source: Zatsiorsky & Kraemer - Science and Practice of Strength Training
